did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically an ensemble scheme of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with fine-tuned deep features, to perform automated classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel ensemble scheme for convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to classify skin lesions, describes the methodology (fine-tuning, feature fusion), and reports original experimental results (AUC of 87.3% and 95.5%) on the ISIC 2017 dataset, demonstrating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Fusing fine-tuned deep features for skin lesion classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for accurate early detection of malignant melanoma by proposing a fully automatic computerized method to classify dermoscopic skin lesion images. The authors introduce a novel ensemble scheme for convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that fuses intra-architecture and inter-architecture features from multiple fine-tuned pre-trained networks. Deep features extracted from these networks are used to train support vector machine classifiers, with final predictions obtained by fusing average prediction probabilities. The method was evaluated on the 600 test images of the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge. The proposed algorithm achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 87.3% for melanoma and 95.5% for seborrheic keratosis classification. These results outperformed the top-ranked methods of the challenge while maintaining a simpler architecture, demonstrating the approach's reliability and robustness.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for classification; the paper focuses on accurate discrimination of malignant skin lesions, specifically melanoma, achieving an AUC of 87.3% on the ISIC 2017 test set.
Primary target class for classification; used as a benign comparator to melanoma, achieving an AUC of 95.5% on the ISIC 2017 test set.

Mentioned in the introduction as a benign lesion that can resemble melanomas and is part of the differential diagnosis context, but not explicitly listed as a target class in the reported experimental results for the ISIC 2017 challenge evaluation.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification for Melanoma (AUC 87.3%) and binary classification for Seborrheic Keratosis (AUC 95.5%) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2017 challenge. The method fuses prediction probabilities from support vector machine classifiers to provide final discrete class predictions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: The dataset used for evaluation in the study, specifically the test set provided by the ISIC 2017 challenge.

use.testing: The proposed algorithm was evaluated on the 600 test images of this dataset to classify melanoma and seborrheic keratosis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are reported as Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) percentages (87.3% for melanoma and 95.5% for seborrheic keratosis) and described qualitatively as 'outperforming' top-ranked methods.

ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification cha.experiment: Proposed ensemble scheme of CNNs with fine-tuned deep features and SVM classifiers
ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification cha.notes: The proposed algorithm outperformed the top-ranked methods of the challenge while maintaining a simpler architecture.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble scheme of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with fine-tuned deep features fused with Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers
architecture_used.pretraining: Multiple pre-trained networks (identical architecture within sets) fine-tuned on dermoscopic skin lesion images

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Deep features extracted from multiple sets of CNNs representing different feature abstraction levels
architecture_details.classifier: Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers trained on the deep features
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Fusion of average prediction probability classification vectors from different sets

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble scheme of CNNs with fine-tuned deep features
conclusions: The proposed ensemble scheme of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with fine-tuned deep features and support vector machine classifiers is a reliable and robust method for the automated classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images.
The method achieves an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 87.3% for melanoma classification and 95.5% for seborrheic keratosis classification on the ISIC 2017 test set.
The proposed algorithm outperforms the top-ranked methods of the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge.
The proposed approach maintains a simpler architecture compared to the top-ranked methods of the challenge while achieving superior performance.
The fusion of intra-architecture and inter-architecture network features effectively represents different feature abstraction levels, contributing to the method's reliability.
important_remarks: The study was evaluated exclusively on the 600 test images of the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge, limiting the assessment to a single external dataset without internal validation or multi-center testing.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to support the claim that the proposed method outperforms top-ranked challenge methods; performance is described qualitatively.

The methodology relies on fine-tuning pre-trained networks on dermoscopic images, which may introduce bias related to the specific distribution of the ISIC 2017 dataset and limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

While the authors claim the architecture is 'simpler' than top-ranked challenge methods, specific comparative metrics regarding model complexity (e.g., parameter count, FLOPs, or inference time) are not provided in the context.

The study focuses only on binary classification tasks for Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis, potentially overlooking the complexity of multi-class differential diagnosis involving benign nevi and other lesion types mentioned in the introduction.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study proposes an ensemble scheme of CNNs with fine-tuned deep features and SVM classifiers for skin lesion classification. The models were fine-tuned on dermoscopic skin lesion images (specifically from the ISIC 2017 training set, though not explicitly named as such in the text, it is implied by the challenge context). The final evaluation was performed exclusively on the 600 test images provided by the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge. No internal validation set or cross-dataset external testing was reported.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report training on one dataset and testing on a different unseen dataset. The evaluation was conducted on the test set of the ISIC 2017 challenge, which is a single held-out dataset for that specific challenge. There is no evidence of cross-dataset validation where the model was trained on one named cohort and tested on another distinct named cohort.

training.notes: The paper states networks were 'fine-tuned on dermoscopic skin lesion images'. Given the evaluation on the ISIC 2017 test set, it is inferred that the training data came from the ISIC 2017 challenge training set, but the specific count or name is not explicitly detailed in the provided text chunks.

validation.notes: No internal validation set is mentioned for model selection or hyperparameter tuning.

testing.notes: The proposed algorithm was evaluated on the 600 test images of the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The method uses 'multiple sets of CNNs of different architecture' that are 'pre-trained networks' fine-tuned on dermoscopic images. The specific pre-training dataset (e.g., ImageNet) is not explicitly named in the provided text, but 'pre-trained' implies a large-scale dataset like ImageNet.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 600 test images from the ISIC 2017 challenge.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The ISIC 2017 test set served as the unseen data for evaluation. No other external datasets were used.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Ensemble scheme of CNNs with fine-tuned deep features and SVM classifiers
dataset_level_results.notes: The results are for binary classification tasks. The method outperformed top-ranked methods of the challenge.

generalizability_notes: The study was evaluated exclusively on the 600 test images of the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge.
This limits the assessment to a single external dataset without internal validation or multi-center testing.

The methodology relies on fine-tuning pre-trained networks on dermoscopic images, which may introduce bias related to the specific distribution of the ISIC 2017 dataset and limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
